title: For I Am Not Able": The Trapping of Achilles
subtitle: Achilles son of Nearchidas also called Cornelius, Hermopolis Magna, c. 160--200 CE
summary: In October 192 CE, a man named Achilles stands before the council of Hermopolis and tries to bargain his way out of an expensive civic office. The minutes preserve the voices of the trap closing around him: the crowd, the lawyer, the strategus, and Achilles himself saying, simply, that he cannot do it.

sections.title: The Trap
sections.body_html:
The scene is a council meeting, and a scribe is recording every word. The record survives on papyrus, now in the John Rylands Library in Manchester. What makes it extraordinary is not the subject matter – a civic appointment, bureaucratic maneuvering – but the fact that we can hear the voices. Not summaries, not descriptions of what was said, but the words themselves, captured as they were spoken in a hall in Middle Egypt on an autumn afternoon in the last year of the Antonine dynasty.

The meeting opens mid-argument. The guild administrators are accusing someone – the name is lost – of “pretending to be without means in order that he may himself go free and other persons be appointed in his place.”2 A man called Harpalus asks for his bail to be released and the keys to his house returned. The guild administrators agree. Then someone goes out. We don’t know who. The papyrus has gaps.

What follows is a new item of business. The people present are listed: Dius, gymnasiarch in office; Dionysius also called something (the alias is lost), exegetes in office; Olympiodorus, advocate; Apollonius son of Heraclapollon, ex-gymnasiarch; and Achilles son of Cornelius.3 These are the great and good of Hermopolis – or, rather, the great and trapped. Every title listed here represents a compulsory civic office. Every man on this list has spent or will spend years and a great deal of money performing unpaid public service.

The crowd – the ordinary citizens standing by – shout out: “Let Achilles be crowned as cosmetes; imitate your father the lover of office, the brave old man.”4

The word “crowned” (stephanoo) is not metaphorical. To crown someone for a liturgical office was to formally nominate them, and once crowned, a man could not easily refuse. The crowd is demanding that Achilles take on the cosmeteia – the office responsible for supervising the gymnasium, the training of the ephebes (young men undergoing their civic education), the festivals, the processions, and the athletics that sustained the Greek cultural life of this Egyptian city.5 It was expensive. The cosmetes paid for much of this out of his own pocket.

Achilles tries a gambit. He speaks:

“Obedient to my native city, I offer to undertake the office of a crowned exegetes on the condition that I contribute an annual sum of two talents and am released from the superintendence of land under lease.”6

This is a counteroffer. The exegetes – literally “the interpreter” – was a different municipal office, higher in prestige but perhaps less onerous in this particular moment.7 Achilles is trying to jump over the cosmeteia to a position he finds less burdensome. He specifies his terms: two talents per year, and exemption from supervising public land leases. He is negotiating.

But Olympiodorus, the advocate – the lawyer in the room – sees through it immediately:

“A person who has offered to undertake the greater office ought not to shun the lesser.”8

The logic is devastating. If you can afford to be exegetes, you can certainly afford to be cosmetes. By making the offer, Achilles has proven his own means.

sections.title: The Interruption
sections.body_html:
Then something remarkable happens. In the middle of this negotiation over a civic office, a man named Ammonion son of Dioscorus interrupts:

“All through this day Achilles struck me, and I will certify these very facts by means of your minutes; for I am petitioning his highness the praefect concerning the insult.”9

The scribe records this too. In the middle of a formal council meeting, a man stands up and accuses Achilles of beating him – earlier that same day. The accusation is entered into the official minutes, which is exactly what Ammonion wants: a certified record for his petition to the prefect.

Achilles denies it: “I neither struck him nor insulted him.”10

The strategus – Sarapion also called Apollonius, the Roman-appointed governor of the nome – intervenes with bureaucratic calm: “What you have said has been recorded; but the cosmetae shall also be summoned in order that you may repeat the same statements in their presence.”11

What are we witnessing? A man under intense public pressure to take on an expensive office, accused of hitting someone that very morning. Was the assault related to the nomination? Had Ammonion been one of those pushing for Achilles to serve? Had there been a confrontation before the meeting? We cannot know. The papyrus records the words but not their causes. What it preserves is the texture of a bad day.

sections.title: The Killing Floor
sections.body_html:
After the interruption, the cosmetae are summoned to the Caesareum – the temple of the imperial cult, which served as a public meeting place.12 They arrive. Their spokesman, Diogenes, speaks:

“We have learned that Achilles in our absence put himself forward for the office of exegetes. This however was not permissible; for the most divine Antoninus ordained by decree that three exegetae-designate are necessary for the office of exegetes.”13

Diogenes reads aloud a copy of the decree of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar – the emperor Marcus Aurelius, father of the reigning Commodus, dead now twelve years but still shaping the bureaucratic life of provincial Egypt.14 The decree established a queue: you needed three designates before someone could move into the exegeteia. There were already enough in the queue. Achilles cannot jump the line.

Then Aspidas, father of Hermas, an ex-cosmetes, stands and says:

“On my personal responsibility I crown Achilles for the office of cosmetes.”15

This is the decisive act. Aspidas takes the nomination upon himself – “on my personal responsibility” (ep’ emas autoprosopou) – meaning that if Achilles defaults or fails, Aspidas will be financially liable. It is a guarantee and a cage at once.

Olympiodorus the advocate delivers the closing argument: “We now have the declaration of Aspidas that he crowns him on his own responsibility; and he ought to be crowned, for the office is now secured to the city.”16

The strategus orders the statements entered into the minutes. The meeting is over.

Achilles is cosmetes.

sections.title: The Father, the Brave Old Man
sections.body_html:
The crowd called Achilles’ father “the lover of office, the brave old man.”17 The father was Nearchidas, also called Cornelius, and the double name tells us something. “Nearchidas” is Greek. “Cornelius” is Roman. In the nomenclature of second-century provincial Egypt, this means the family moved in the upper stratum of Hermopolitan society, where Greek civic identity and Roman legal status overlapped.18

We catch glimpses of this family in earlier documents. In 93 CE – a full century before Achilles’ bad day at the Caesareum – a man named “Eudaimon the Elder, son of Hermaios, Hermopolite, and Nearchas son of Eudaimon, Hermopolite” registered their donkeys for tax purposes.19 Eudaimon had three female donkeys and a male and female foal. Nearchas had one female donkey, “not for hire, but for personal use.” They lived in the quarter of the Western Fortress, and they owned land.20

This Nearchas son of Eudaimon is very likely the grandfather – or great-uncle – of the Nearchidas also called Cornelius who fathered Achilles. The family had been property-owning Hermopolitans for at least three generations. In a fragmentary letter from the late first or early second century, a Nearchas writes to a Eudaimon about agricultural leases – arouras of land, rent agreements, seed loans.21 In another document from 85–86 CE, a pigeon-coop lease mentions a Nearchas receiving pigeons as rent: one hundred per year.22

The family owned land. They kept donkeys and pigeon-coops. They dealt in grain leases. By the late second century, this quiet accumulation of provincial wealth had produced a man – Achilles – who was rich enough to be dangerous: wealthy enough to be nominated for civic office, but not wealthy enough (or not willing enough) to embrace the nomination without a fight.

sections.title: The Cosmetes and His Colleagues
sections.body_html:
The man who crowned Achilles – Aspidas, ex-cosmetes – may or may not be the same Aspidas who appears in a document from 69 CE as an estate manager.23 We cannot be sure; the name is not common, but the dates are far apart.

What we can trace with more confidence is Diogenes, the spokesman of the cosmetae who quoted Marcus Aurelius’s decree to block Achilles. His full name was Diogenes son of Hermaeus, and his life can be pieced together from several documents spanning decades.

In 171 CE – twenty-one years before the council meeting – a woman from the Cornelius family (a daughter of Hexakon son of Cornelius, registered at the Western Guard-house) sold eleven arouras of catoecic land to “Hermaeus son of Diogenes.”24 This Hermaeus was almost certainly the son of the very Diogenes who would later become cosmetes – or perhaps a relative, given the patronymic. The buyer and the seller belonged to the same small world of Hermopolitan property owners, the same web of families who bought land from one another, intermarried, witnessed each other’s contracts, and – eventually – nominated each other for offices nobody wanted.

By 185 CE, Diogenes son of Hermaios had served as cosmetes and was carrying out further public duties: in a receipt from the village of Skordon, he and Herminos son of Herodianos, an ex-agoranomos, are recorded as having been “appointed by the former acting strategus” to handle barley distribution from a public bank.25 Two years later, in 187 CE, the same pair – Diogenes son of Hermaios, ex-cosmetes, and Herminos son of Herodianos, ex-agoranomos – were again appointed to manage grain distribution for a cavalry regiment.26

Having served as cosmetes, Diogenes was not free. He was still being assigned tasks, still collecting grain, still responsible for public money. This is the trap that Achilles could see coming. These offices did not end. One service led to another. Your wealth marked you as capable, and capability was conscription.

Three years after the council meeting, in 195 CE, we find Diogenes still active: a receipt records that the city treasurer paid 5,600 silver drachmas “to the account of the horse-races” to the “body of the cosmetae acting through Diogenes and Agathodaemon, ex-cosmetae.”27 Horse-races. Five thousand six hundred drachmas. The cosmetae were responsible for the city’s festival calendar – the athletics, the processions, the races – and the money came from the municipal treasury, but the cosmetae managed it and often supplemented it from their own pockets. Hermopolis maintained horse-races in the last years of Commodus and into the reign of Septimius Severus. The show went on.

sections.title: What Happened After
sections.body_html:
Diogenes eventually died, as all the men in this story did, and we catch one last glimpse of his family’s affairs in a document from after 204 CE. His grandsons – Sarapion and Hermes, called Marcullus, and their half-brother Achilles (a different Achilles, son of Polydeuces) – addressed a document to “our uncles on our mother’s side, sons of Diogenes, ex-cosmetes of the said city of Hermopolis.”28

The grandsons’ mother, Victorina, had died. While Diogenes was alive, he had arranged for his sons to provide an annual allowance to Victorina and her descendants in perpetuity. After her death, the grandsons agreed to accept a lump settlement of 20 artabas of wheat and release the sons of Diogenes from any further claims “henceforth for ever.”29

Twenty artabas of wheat, and the obligation ends. Families negotiate, compromise, settle. The allowance that Diogenes set up while alive – the written deed specifying payments from the sons to the daughter – is the kind of arrangement a careful man makes. It is the work of someone who understood property, obligation, and the value of putting things in writing. This was the man who quoted imperial decrees from memory in the council hall.

sections.title: The Machinery of Reluctance
sections.body_html:
Achilles was not unique. The papyri preserve an entire bureaucratic genre of reluctance – men trying to escape civic office and being herded back into it by their peers.

A century later, in Oxyrhynchus, a council meeting records the same dynamic with a different victim. Ptolemaeus son of Damarion, a chief priest, begs not to be made a public banker:

“I entreat you, I cannot serve. I am a man of moderate means, I live in my father’s house…”30

The prytanis pushes back: “Even if Ptolemaeus… he cannot refuse you with regard to the post of public banker.” The senators chant: “Ptolemaeus will not refuse his tribe.” It is, again, a trap made of words, of public pressure, of collective need dressed up as flattery. “Upright, faithful Ptolemaeus,” they call him – praising the man they are ruining.31

Officials absconded. The same Oxyrhynchus minutes record two other men – Actiasion and Theon – who “had absconded” from their duties convoying tax collectors. The senators’ response to the news is darkly funny: “Let the replacement not be nominated beforehand, lest they run away too.”32

This was the system. The Roman administration of Egypt devolved expensive and time-consuming tasks onto local elites through “liturgies” – compulsory public services.33 A man of sufficient property was nominated by his peers, and once nominated, he served or he fled, and if he fled, his guarantor paid. The offices were many: gymnasiarch, cosmetes, exegetes, agoranomos, tax collector, grain supervisor, canal maintainer, banker. Each one cost money and time. Each one was unavoidable for anyone with enough land, enough grain, enough donkeys.

The scholar Naphtali Lewis described the cumulative effect: “decades of economic recession, further aggravated by ever-increasing taxes and assessments and by requisitions imposed to sustain the internecine struggles over the imperial succession and the wars on the empire’s frontiers, succeeded in undermining the fortunes of many a well-to-do family.”34 By the third century, the system was devouring the class it depended on. But already in 192 CE, when Achilles stood in the Caesareum and said that he was not able to support the office of cosmetes, the strain was visible.35

sections.title: "The Delightful Praefecture of Larcius Memor"
sections.body_html:
One detail in the council minutes is easy to miss. When Olympiodorus the advocate is making his case for why Achilles should serve, he invokes the political moment:

“The fortune of our lord the Emperor provides offices abundantly and augments the prosperity of the city; how can it not, during the delightful praefecture of Larcius Memor?”36

The emperor is Commodus. The year is 192. In two months, Commodus will be dead – strangled in his bath by a wrestler named Narcissus, after a poisoning attempt by his concubine Marcia failed when he vomited up the dose.37 The “delightful praefecture” of Larcius Memor will end. The Year of the Five Emperors will begin. Civil war will come.

But in October 192 CE, none of this has happened yet. In Hermopolis Magna, in the great temple of the imperial cult, they are still flattering the emperor and praising the prefect, still filling offices and running horse-races, still keeping the machinery of Greek civic life turning in an Egyptian city eight hundred kilometers up the Nile from Rome. Olympiodorus speaks of augmented prosperity. Perhaps he believes it. Perhaps it is the kind of thing one says.

Achilles said he was not able. They crowned him anyway.

sections.title: What the Record Does Not Say
sections.body_html:
We do not know how Achilles served as cosmetes, or for how long. We do not know whether the expense ruined him or merely diminished him. We do not know whether Ammonion ever filed his petition about the assault, or what the prefect made of it, or whether the two men ever spoke again.

We do not know what Achilles thought when word came, sometime in early 193 CE, that Commodus was dead and a new man sat in the purple. The “delightful praefecture” was over. The flattery of October would need new objects.

What we have is a single afternoon in the Caesareum: the shout of the crowd, the maneuver of the advocate, the interruption of the aggrieved man, the crowning by the guarantor, the scribe recording it all. And a voice saying, in a public building in Hermopolis, “for I am not able” – and being told, by the collective force of a city that needed him more than it cared about his consent: you are able enough.

The modern reader who has been volunteered for a committee, nominated for a role they did not seek, praised into a responsibility they cannot refuse – they recognize this. The technology of the trap changes. The trap does not.

notes.text: P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841), dated 31 October 192 CE, reign of Commodus (year 32). Published in A. S. Hunt and C. C. Edgar, *Catalogue of the Greek Papyri in the John Rylands Library, Manchester*, vol. 2 (1915). The document contains official minutes of a council meeting at Hermopolis Magna, preserved with verbatim dialogue. The translation used throughout is that of Hunt and Edgar.
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841), opening section. The beginning of the papyrus is damaged, and the name of the person accused of feigning poverty is lost.

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841). The presence list names the officials at the council meeting: Dius (gymnasiarch in office), Dionysius also called [name lost] (exegetes in office), Olympiodorus (advocate), Apollonius son of Heraclapollon (ex-gymnasiarch), and Achilles son of Cornelius.
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "the citizens standing by cried out 'Let Achilles be crowned as cosmetes; imitate your father the lover of office, the brave old man.'"

On the office of cosmetes in Roman Egypt, see P. Jesenko, "Der Kosmet: Eine Studie zu einem liturgischen Amt des kaiserzeitlichen Agypten" (2012). The cosmetes supervised the ephebeia (training of citizen youth), maintained the gymnasium, and organized festivals. Around 190 documents -- papyri, inscriptions, ostraca -- attest the office from the 1st to the mid-4th century CE.

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "Obedient to my native city, I offer to undertake the office of a crowned exegetes on the condition that I contribute an annual sum of two talents and am released from the superintendence of land under lease."

The exegetes ("interpreter") was a senior municipal magistrate responsible for legal interpretation and civic governance. In the hierarchy of Hermopolitan offices, the exegetes ranked above the cosmetes; see N. Lewis, *The Compulsory Public Services of Roman Egypt* (1997).
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): Olympiodorus said, "A person who has offered to undertake the greater office ought not to shun the lesser."

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "Ammonion son of Dioscorus, interrupting, said: 'All through this day Achilles struck me, and I will certify these very facts by means of your minutes; for I am petitioning his highness the praefect concerning the insult.'"
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "Achilles said: 'I neither struck him nor insulted him.'"

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "Sarapion also called Apollonius, strategus, said: 'What you have said has been recorded; but the cosmetae shall also be summoned in order that you may repeat the same statements in their presence.'"

The Caesareum -- a temple dedicated to the imperial cult -- served as a civic meeting space in many Egyptian cities. On the Caesareum at Hermopolis, see D. M. Bailey, "Excavations at El-Ashmunein" (British Museum, 1991 ff.).

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): Diogenes said, "We have learned that Achilles in our absence put himself forward for the office of exegetes. This however was not permissible; for the most divine Antoninus ordained by decree that three exegetae-designate are necessary for the office of exegetes."

The "most divine Antoninus" is Marcus Aurelius (r. 161--180 CE), who issued numerous edicts affecting municipal governance in Egypt. His decree establishing the queue of three exegetae-designate would have been well known to civic officials in Hermopolis, where copies were kept and cited years after his death.
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): "Aspidas father of Hermas, ex-cosmetes, who was present said: 'On my personal responsibility I crown Achilles for the office of cosmetes.'"

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): Olympiodorus said, "We now have the declaration of Aspidas that he crowns him on his own responsibility; and he ought to be crowned, for the office is now secured to the city."

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841). The crowd's praise of Achilles' father as "the lover of office, the brave old man" translates the Greek *ton philarkhon, ton kalon presbuton* and suggests Nearchidas also called Cornelius had served willingly and generously in civic offices -- setting a standard that his son was now expected to meet.

On double names (Greek/Roman) in Roman Egypt as markers of social status, see R. Bagnall, *Egypt in Late Antiquity* (1993), pp. 232--240. The formal identification "Nearchidas also called Cornelius" appears in the final section of P.Ryl. 2.77 where the magistrates write to the strategus. The Roman cognomen "Cornelius" indicates the family likely held Roman citizenship or had close connections to Roman administrative culture.

PSI 7.785 (corpus ID 37888), dated 23 February 93 CE. A donkey registration filed by "Eudaimon the Elder, son of Hermaios, Hermopolite, and Nearchas son of Eudaimon, Hermopolite." The registration is for the 12th year of Domitian. They declare their donkeys: Eudaimon has three female donkeys and two foals; Nearchas has one female donkey "not for hire, but for personal use." They reside in the quarter of the Western Fortress.

PSI 7.785 (corpus ID 37888). The statement that Nearchas's donkey was "not for hire" (*ou misthou*) indicates the animal was for the family's own agricultural and transport needs -- a minor detail that confirms their status as working landowners rather than commercial animal-renters.

PSI 8.974 (corpus ID 38278), dated late 1st to early 2nd century CE. A fragmentary letter from Nearchas to Eudaimon about agricultural leases -- arouras of land, rents (*ekphorion*), seeds. The letter discusses negotiating lease terms and mentions "his sons."

P.Flor. 3.361 (corpus ID 38113), dated 85--86 CE. A pigeon-coop lease mentioning Nearchas: "the pigeons shall be one hundred per year... the manure produced until the end of the term shall belong to me, Nearchas." Pigeon-keeping was common in the Fayum and Middle Egypt, and manure was a valuable by-product used as fertilizer.

P.Oxy. 75.5050 (corpus ID 44087), dated 26 February 69 CE: "Aspidas, estate-manager of C. Prosius Hospes, to Sarapion, son of Diogenes, greetings." This Aspidas from Oxyrhynchus is over a century earlier than the Hermopolitan Aspidas who crowned Achilles and probably a different person. The name, however, is uncommon.

P.Ryl. 2.164 (corpus ID 38163), dated 3 November 171 CE. A land sale from a woman described as "daughter of Hexakon son of Cornelius, of Hermopolis, registered at the Western Guard-house" to "Hermaeus son of Diogenes of the same city." The seller's husband is "Metocus son of Achilles." The connection to the Cornelius family and the appearance of both "Achilles" and "Hermaeus son of Diogenes" suggest this document belongs to the same social network as P.Ryl. 2.77, though identifications across documents are tentative -- these were common names in Hermopolis.

Chrest.Wilck. 418 (corpus ID 137133), dated August--November 185 CE. Village elders of Skordon in the upper Patemite acknowledge receiving barley from "Diogenes son of Hermaios, former kosmetes, and Hermeinos son of Herodianos, former agoranomos, of Hermoupolis the great, who were appointed by the former acting strategos Ammonios."

BGU 3.842 (corpus ID 38072), dated 15 September 187 CE. Addressed to "Diogenes son of Hermaios, ex-cosmetes of Hermopolis the Great, and Herminos son of Herodianos, ex-agoranomos of the same city," recording grain distributions to a cavalryman, Cocceus Martialis, in the 28th year of Commodus.

P.Ryl. 2.86 (corpus ID 37842), dated 16 November 195 CE. "Dionysius son of Archias, ex-gymnasiarch of Hermopolis Magna, treasurer of the municipal and priestly funds, to the body of the cosmetae acting through Diogenes and Agathodaemon, ex-cosmetae, greeting. I have paid to-day... to the account of the horse-races of the said 4th year, five thousand six hundred silver drachmae." The 4th year is of Septimius Severus.

P.Ryl. 2.181 (corpus ID 37831), after 204 CE. The heirs of Victorina -- Sarapion, Hermes called Marcullus, and their half-brother Achilles (a different Achilles, son of Polydeuces) -- address "our uncles on our mother's side, sons of Diogenes, ex-cosmetes of the said city of Hermopolis." Diogenes son of Hermaeus had arranged an annual allowance for his daughter Victorina and her descendants from the 8th year of Septimius Severus and Caracalla (199/200 CE).
P.Ryl. 2.181 (corpus ID 37831): "we acknowledge that we have here received from you the amount agreed upon in discharge of all the said allowances for ever." The settlement was 20 artabas of wheat.

P.Oxy. 12.1415 (corpus ID 22162), c. 280--300 CE. Council minutes from Oxyrhynchus: "Ptolemaeus son of Damarion, chief-priest, said: 'I entreat you, I cannot serve. I am a man of moderate means, I live in my father's house.'"

P.Oxy. 12.1415 (corpus ID 22162): "The senators said, 'Upright, faithful Ptolemaeus...'" and "Eudaemon, exegetes, said, 'Ptolemaeus has often given an example of his loyalty.'" The praise functions as coercion -- to refuse after being called faithful is to prove oneself unfaithful.
P.Oxy. 12.1415 (corpus ID 22162): concerning replacements for Actiasion and Theon "who had absconded," the senators said, "Let the replacement not be nominated beforehand, lest they run away."

On the system of compulsory liturgies in Roman Egypt, see N. Lewis, *The Compulsory Public Services of Roman Egypt*, Papyrologica Florentina vol. 28 (1997); also N. Lewis, *Leitourgia Papyri: Documents on Compulsory Public Service in Egypt under Roman Rule*, Transactions of the American Philosophical Society 53.9 (1963).
N. Lewis, *The Compulsory Public Services of Roman Egypt* (1997), describing the cumulative economic effect of the liturgical system on provincial elites.
P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): Achilles said, "for I am not able to support the office of cosmetes" -- Greek *ou gar dynamai ten kosmeteian hyposteinai*.

P.Ryl. 2.77 (corpus ID 37841): Olympiodorus said, "The fortune of our lord the Emperor provides offices abundantly and augments the prosperity of the city; how can it not, during the delightful praefecture of Larcius Memor?"

On the assassination of Commodus on 31 December 192 CE, see Cassius Dio 73.22 and Herodian 1.17. The conspirators were the emperor's concubine Marcia, the Praetorian Prefect Laetus, and the freedman Eclectus. The wrestler Narcissus strangled Commodus after the poison failed. The Year of the Five Emperors followed. Larcius Memor was prefect of Egypt in 192 CE; his tenure is attested in P.Ryl. 2.77 itself, among other papyri.

The administrators of the guilds said :— “... has made a pretence of being without means
               
            
            
               
                    in order that he may himself go free and other persons be appointed in his place.” Harpalus said:— “If you think good, order the bail which I provided to be released and the keys of the house to be restored.” The administrators of the guilds said:— “The bail shall be released and you shall receive the keys.” . . . as, assistant, went out. Thus far the copy. The 32nd year, the 29th of the month Epeiph. I pray for your health, dear friends.—And a copy of minutes:— ...there being present at the tribune Dius, gymnasiarch in office, Dionysius also called . . ., exegetes in office, Olympiodorus, advocate, Apollonius son of Heraclapollon, ex-gymnasiarch, and Achilles son of Cornelius, the citizens standing by cried out “Let Achilles be crowned as cosmetes; imitate your father the lover of office, the brave old man”, whereupon Achilles said:— “Obedient to my native city, I offer to undertake the office of a crowned exegetes on the condition that I contribute an annual sum of two talents and am released from the superintendence of land under lease.” Olympiodorus said:— “The fortune of our lord the Emperor provides offices abundantly and augments the prosperity of the city; how can it not, during the delightful praefecture of Larcius Memor? If then Achilles wishes to be crowned as exegetes, let him pay the entrance deposit (?). Otherwise he none the less nominated himself for the office immediately required, that of cosmetes.” Achilles said:- I offered to to undertake the office of exegetes on the condition that I contribute two talents annually; for I am not able to support the office of cosmetes.” Olympiodorus said:— “A person who has offered to undertake the greater office ought not to shun the lesser.” Ammonion son of Dioscorus, interrupting, said:— “All through this day Achilles struck me, and I will certify these very facts by means of your minutes; for I am petitioning his highness the praefect concerning the insult.” Achilles said: “I neither struck him nor insulted him.” Sarapion also called Apollonius, strategus, said :— “What you have said has been recorded; but the cosmetae shall also be summoned in order that you may repeat the same statements in their presence.” After a while Diogenes and Dioscorus and their fellow cosmetae came forward at the Caesareum in the presence of Achilles, and one of them, their spokesman Diogenes, said :— “We have learned that Achilles in our absence put himself forward for the office of exegetes. This however was not permissible; for the most divine Antoninus ordained by decree that three exegetae-designate (?) are necessary for the office of exegetes, and when, therefore, there are many designate he ought to pass on to the office immediately required; and I will read the decree to you to that effect.” When he had read a copy of the decree of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar, Aspidas father of Hermas, ex-cosmetes, who was present said :— “On my personal responsibility I crown Achilles for the office of cosmetes.” Olympiodorus said: “We now have the declaration of Aspidas that he crowns him on his own responsibility ; and he ought to be crowned, for the office is now secured to the city.' The strategus directed the statements to be entered on the minutes. Read by me.—A copy of a further missive:—The magistrates of Hermopolis the Great to their dearest Sarapion also called Apollonius, strategus of the Hermopolite nome, greeting. Achilles son of Nearchidas also called Cornelius, ex-agoranomus, on being presented to the office of cosmetes by certain cosmetae offered in your presence to become exegetes. But when we urged him to undertake the office of cosmetes, since the city had not many cosmetae while the exegetae-designate were more numerous, Aspidas father of Hermas, ex-cosmetes, crowned him as cosmetes on his own responsibility, as was inserted in your minutes. Since, therefore, the office is secured to the city through one or other of them, notice is given you in order that you may arrange to carry out the consequences of the proceedings before you, so that the city may obtain the magistracy. The 32nd year, Pharmouthi 13. The exegetes and gymnasiarch signed.

sources.translation: ‘Dionysius son of Archias, ex-gymnasiarch of Hermopolis Magna, treasurer of the municipal and priestly funds, to the body of the cosmetae acting through Diogenes and Agathodaemon, ex-cosmetae, greeting. I have paid to-day, being the 19th day of the month Hathur of the present year, to the account of the horse-races of the said 4th year, five thousand six hundred silver drachmae, total 5,600 silver dr., without prejudice to any right claimed by the city.’ Date and signatures.

sources.translation: To Damarion, strategos of the Hermopolite nome. Nepheros son of Phibis, mother T[...]tos, about 35 years old, scar on the nose, and the remaining elders of the village of Skordon of the upper Patemite, of the current year 26 of Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Caesar the lord, through me the writer and aforementioned person. We acknowledge that we have received from Diogenes son of Hermaios, former kosmetes, and Hermeinos son of Herodianos, former agoranomos, of Hermoupolis the great, who were appointed by the former acting strategos Ammonios, the price of the barley to be collected from the public bank and distributed to those who provided the price of the barley assessed upon our village, from the harvest of the past year 25: [...] artabas of barley, in accordance with the assessment made by the financial officials of the nome, as the [...] of drachmas [...] two hundred eighty [...].

sources.translation: ‘… arion daughter of Hexakon son of Cornelius, of Hermopolis, registered at the Western Guard-house, my mother being Olympias … with my guardian my husband Metocus son of Achilles, to Hermaeus son of Diogenes of the same city, greeting. I acknowledge that I have sold to you from the present day for ever the 11… arurae of catoecic land not subject to demand belonging to me in the ... at Tertesmonis in the holding of Asclepiodorus, together with their yearly rentals, and will transfer these severally in good condition for sowing, in full, measured by the just measure used in the settlement, free from the cultivation of royal land and every impost whatever, contained in 4¾ common and undivided plots in which are watering-places and a quay of baked brick, also common, at the price mutually agreed upon of … drachmae. From this sum I have here received from you from hand to hand three thousand drachmae, total 3,000 silver dr. The remaining … drachmae you will pay me by the 30th of the present month Hathur of the current 12th year of Aurelius Antoninus the lord, and I will make the conveyance whensoever you please by an official deed through the record-offices in Hermopolis in order that you may receive the authorization of the land-registry office free from all liability. For the public dues on the holding and all additional levies from former times up to the past 11th year of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Caesar the lord I, the vendor, am responsible, while you are responsible for those from the present 12th year inclusive; and I will henceforth guarantee the said land with every guarantee. The land-specifications and the conditions of sale will be included in the deed of conveyance.’ Date, signatures of the writer (?), of the vendor and her guardian, and possibly of the purchaser.

‘Sarapion and Hermes also called Marcullus, both sons of Hermes, and their brother on the mother’s
side, Achilles son of Polydeuces, minor, all three sons and heirs of Victorina, the said minor Achilles acting through his father Polydeuces, all four being citizens of Hermopolis, to Herm … and … and …, our three uncles on our mother's side, sons of Diogenes, ex-cosmetes of the said city of Hermopolis, greeting. Whereas your father Diogenes son of Hermaeus while yet alive desired, according to his written deed, that a certain annual allowance should be furnished in perpetuity from the 8th year of the lords and Emperors Severus and Antoninus to our mother his daughter Victorina and her descendants by you his sons, and after Diogenes’ death you furnished the allowance specified by the deed until the 12th year; and whereas on the death of our mother Victorina we have now agreed with you to receive a fixed amount of 20 artabae in discharge of all allowances for the future; we acknowledge that we have here received from you the amount agreed upon in discharge of all the said allowances for ever, and that we make and shall make no claim against you or your assigns on any point whatever concerning the said allowances henceforth for ever in any manner or under any pretext, because we have come to the present agreement, and any person making a claim upon you in our name with regard to the allowance we will repel …’

sources.translation:
To Damarion, strategus of the Hermopolite nome, from Antonius Justinus, duplicarius, dispatched 
by Valerius Frontinus, praefect of the ala Heracliana stationed at Coptos. I have had measured to me by the 
elders of the village of Magdola Petechontos in the upper Koussite district for the past 25th year of Aurelius 
Commodus Antoninus Caesar the lord the proportion imposed upon them from the twenty thousand artabae of 
barley which his highness the praefect Longaeus Rufus ordered to be bought up from the produce of the 
twenty-third year for the requirements of the aforesaid ala, of barley, measured by the official standard at the 
appointed time for measuring ... . artabae in accordance with the division made by the officials of the nome; 
and I have issued four copies of this receipt. Date.
Translation: P.Ryl. 2 85

Translation: from P.Oxy. 12
              The prytanis said, “... and it is read to you.” Two communications from the strategus having been read, one concerning the appointment of a substitute for Actiasion, senator (?), son of ..., convoying collectors of wine, who had absconded, the other concerning the appointment likewise of a substitute for Theon, senator, son of ..., convoying from Arabia (?) collectors of barley, who had absconded, after the reading the prytanis said, “... Appoint persons to do the duty, in order that the carriage of the annona for the most noble soldiers may not be hindered.” The senators said, “Let ... not be (nominated) beforehand ..., lest they run away.” The prytanis said, “On this point we will refer to his excellency the epistrategus, and we will also add a statement concerning ..., and concerning a collector of wine in place of Aristion, who has been released by his excellency the epistrategus in another memorandum.” The senators said, “... Aristion, when he comes, will prosper; a prosperous public servant was Aristion.” The prytanis said, “Appoint the persons to office, in order that there may be no delay ...” The senators said, “The surety of ... was already (?) deposited with the strategus, the sureties of Actiasion and Theon were deposited with the strategus.” The prytanis said, ...
              The prytanis said, “... and it is read to you.” A communication from the strategus having been read concerning ..., and one from the banker of public moneys concerning the appointment of a substitute for Pasion, who ..., after the reading the senators said, “Let the prytanis nominate a richer person.” The prytanis said, ...
              ... Communications from ...esus having been read concerning ..., after the reading the prytanis said, “... were appointed to a public office although they already held such offices.” The senators said, ... The prytanis said, “Fill up the office, appoint ..., nominate whom you will.” The members of the .. tribe said, ... Eudaemon, exegetes, said (?), “Ptolemaeus (cannot?) remain in the public office ...” Ptolemaeus son of Damarion, chief-priest, said, “I entreat you, I cannot serve. I am aman of moderate means, I live in my father’s house, ...” The prytanis said, “Ptolemaeus still requires to be pressed by you, for he too shrinks from so great an office...” Eudaemon, exegetes, said, “Ptolemaeus too is a man of moderate means and unable to bear the burden ...” Ptolemaeus son of Damarion,chief-priest, said, “The office is beyond my powers.” The prytanis said, “Even if Ptolemaeus ... in another office (?), he cannot refuse you with regard to the post of public banker. It is, however, clear that it is not permitted ...” The senators said, “Upright, faithful Ptolemaeus ...” Ptolemaeus son of Damarion, chief-priest, said, “I entreat you, I cannot undertake two public offices at one time...” Eudaemon, exegetes, said, “Ptolemaeus has often given an example of his loyalty.” The prytanis said, “... I propose him to you.” The senators said, “Ptolemaeus will not refuse his tribe...” Eudaemon, exegetes, said, “You elected him on account of his good faith.”

sources.translation: Translation: from P.Oxy. 1 After an elaborate introduction, in which the council of Oxyrhynchus through its president Aurelius Apollo addresses the strategus Aurelius Apollonius through Asclepiades, the papyrus proceeds (line 8):—At a meeting of our body a dispatch was read from Theodorus who was recently chosen in place of Arion the scribe to proceed to his highness the praefect and attend his immaculate court. In this dispatch he explained that he is a victor in the games and exempt from inquiries ... We have therefore nominated Aurelius ... to serve, and we send you word accordingly, in order that this fact may be brought to his knowledge, and no time be lost in his departure and attendance upon the court. We pray for your health, dearest brother.

sources.translation:
Panechotes also called Panares, ex-cosmetes of Oxyrhynchus,
                     through his friend Gemellus, to Apollonius, writer of shorthand, greeting. I have placed with you
                     my slave Chaerammon to be taught the signs which your son Dionysius knows,
                     for a period of two years dating from the present month Phamenoth of the
                     18th year of Antoninus Caesar the lord at the salary agreed upon
                     between us, 120 silver drachmae, not including feast-days;
                     of which sum you have received the first instalment amounting to 40 drachmae,
                     and you will receive the second instalment consisting of 40 drachmae when the boy has learnt the whole system,
                     and the third you will receive at the end of the period
                     when the boy writes fluently in every respect and reads faultlessly,
                     viz. the remaining 40 drachmae.  If you make him perfect within the period,
                     I will not wait for the aforesaid limit; but it is not lawful
                     for me to take the boy away before the end of the period, and he shall remain with you after the expiration of it
                     for as many days or months as he may have done no work. The 18th year of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus
                     Antoninus Augustus Pius, Phamenoth 5.
                     Translation: B.P. Grenfell – A.S. Hunt, P.Oxy. 4 724

sources.translation:
Aspidas, estate-manager of C. Prosius Hospes, to Sarapion, son of Diogenes, greetings. I have
received from you the money for the axon that I used to keep with you, and I have no claim against
you. In the first year of Marcus Otho Caesar Augustus Imperator, Phamenoth 2. (A. Hartmann)
